name: San Antonio Mountains
short_description: BLM public land in remote Nye County featuring quartz, chalcedony, and jasper in desert washes. Free collecting with 25 lb daily limit makes this an accessible spot for rockhounds.
description:
The San Antonio Mountains in Nye County offer excellent rockhounding opportunities on public BLM land with no permit requirement. This remote high desert location sits in the Basin and Range province where ancient volcanic activity and mineral-rich groundwater created abundant quartz, chalcedony, and jasper deposits. The terrain combines rocky foothills and desert washes where collectors regularly find specimens ranging from small crystals to substantial chunks suitable for cutting and polishing.

Collectors should expect a rugged desert environment with sparse vegetation typical of Nye County's high desert climate. The area receives minimal rainfall, keeping ground conditions dry and accessible most of the year. Elevation ranges from 4,500 to 5,500 feet, making temperature extremes less intense than lower desert locations but still requiring water and sun protection.

The best collecting occurs in washes and along hillside outcrops where erosion naturally exposes mineral deposits. Surface collecting is straightforward - no digging or excavation is permitted on BLM land. The location rewards explorers who are willing to hike between washes and examine multiple exposures. Most productive areas lie within 1 to 3 miles of reliable parking pullouts.

This site is ideal for rockhounders seeking a remote collecting experience without permit hassles. The 25 lb daily limit per person encourages selective collecting and helps preserve the resource. Bring sturdy boots for rocky terrain, abundant water for the desert climate, and be prepared for isolation far from town services.
history: The San Antonio Mountains lie within Nevada's Basin and Range physiographic province, where Tertiary-age volcanic activity (roughly 20 to 40 million years ago) deposited silica-rich rhyolitic flows and related hydrothermal systems. These geological conditions created the extensive quartz and chalcedony deposits found throughout the range, while subsurface mineral-bearing fluids altered surrounding host rocks into colorful jasper and agate formations that collectors find today.
tips: Focus your search in dry washes and arroyo beds where seasonal runoff naturally concentrates loose specimens - walk these features first before ascending hillsides. Look for translucent or milky quartz fragments and banded chalcedony pieces weathering from pale tan to deep burgundy; jasper typically appears as opaque brown or rust-colored chunks on the surface. Bring a small hand lens to identify quality material in the field and a sturdy geology hammer or chisel for collecting samples from accessible outcrops. Early morning searches reward you with better visibility and cooler temperatures - the desert heat intensifies quickly at this elevation.
terrain_notes: High desert terrain at 4,500 to 5,500 feet elevation with rolling rocky foothills and scattered piñon-juniper woodland. Terrain is moderately steep in places with abundant loose rock, small boulders, and sharp volcanic material. Washes are typically dry but show evidence of seasonal flash flooding. Walking distance from parking to productive collecting areas ranges from 0.5 to 3 miles. Footing is uneven and rocky throughout - sturdy boots are essential.
written_directions: From Pahrump, take Nevada State Route 160 north toward Tonopah for approximately 35 miles. Continue north on US 95 toward the San Antonio Mountains area for another 8 to 10 miles. Look for BLM access points and informal dirt pullouts along the east side of US 95 where the San Antonio Mountains come close to the highway. Park safely off the main road and hike into washes and rocky foothills. Multiple entry points exist along this 15-mile stretch; scout several locations to find the most productive sites.
road_conditions: US 95 is paved. Secondary access roads and parking pullouts are unpaved dirt or gravel, passable for standard passenger vehicles in dry conditions. Some rough sections may challenge low-clearance vehicles after heavy rain.
parking_notes: Informal dirt pullouts and wide shoulders along US 95 and secondary roads. No developed parking lot. Space for 2 to 4 vehicles at most pullouts. Arrive early during peak season to secure a spot.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA (Federal Land Policy and Management Act). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or mechanized equipment. No collection of fossils or specimens from protected areas. Removal of rockhounding material is the only permitted use; no other resource extraction is allowed.
rules:
Personal use collecting only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per person
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or power tools
No fossil collection
No removal of archaeological artifacts
nearest_services: Pahrump, Nevada - approximately 35 miles south. Full services including gas, food, water, and supplies. No services near the rockhounding site itself.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky terrain with scattered boulders and loose volcanic material. No wheelchair access. Terrain ranges from gentle to moderately steep. Multiple washes offer easier walking than hillsides. Sturdy hiking boots strongly recommended. Bring more water than you think necessary - this is remote high desert with no water sources nearby.

faq.question: What can I find rockhounding in the San Antonio Mountains near Pahrump?
faq.answer: The San Antonio Mountains on BLM land yield quartz, chalcedony, jasper, and agate specimens. Most material is found as surface fragments in washes and along hillside outcrops. Chalcedony here ranges from pale tan to deep burgundy, while jasper appears in opaque brown and rust tones. Quality material suitable for cutting and polishing is regularly available for collectors who explore multiple washes.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound the San Antonio Mountains in Nevada?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding on BLM land in the San Antonio Mountains. Personal use collection is free under Nevada and federal regulations. You are limited to 25 lbs per day per person. Commercial collection, motorized excavation, and fossil removal are prohibited.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to collect quartz and chalcedony in the San Antonio Mountains?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal for San Antonio Mountains rockhounding. Winter temperatures are mild at this 4,500 to 5,500 foot elevation, and the dry desert climate keeps ground conditions accessible year-round. Summer heat (June - September) can exceed 95 degrees Fahrenheit and makes extended hiking uncomfortable. Spring and fall offer the most pleasant collecting conditions.

faq.question: How far is the San Antonio Mountains from Pahrump and what kind of vehicle do I need?
faq.answer: The San Antonio Mountains are approximately 35 miles north of Pahrump via Nevada State Route 160 and US 95. A standard passenger vehicle is adequate to reach pullouts and parking areas along US 95. Dirt access roads are unpaved but generally passable in dry conditions. High-clearance vehicles are helpful but not required.

faq.question: Are the San Antonio Mountains suitable for beginning rockhounders?
faq.answer: Yes, the San Antonio Mountains are beginner-friendly for rockhounds aged 8 and up. The site requires no permits, has straightforward surface collecting, and produces visible material you can identify in the field. Terrain is moderately challenging with loose rock, so sturdy boots and careful footing are necessary. The remote location demands self-sufficiency and good preparation, but the actual collecting technique is simple.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring rockhounding in the San Antonio Mountains?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots, a small hand lens for field identification, a geology hammer or chisel for sampling outcrops, and a collection bag or backpack. Essential supplies include 2 to 3 liters of water per person, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and a map. The area is remote with spotty cell service, so tell someone your plans. Bring a headlamp if you plan to explore washes near dusk.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Pahrump for quartz, chalcedony, and jasper. No permit needed, 25 lb daily limit. Remote high desert collecting.
